cluster.title: Word Families & Patterns — Kefiw
cluster.subhead: Find words by prefix, suffix, or letter shape.
cluster.intro: English words cluster into families by how they start, how they end, and which letters repeat. This cluster groups the prefix, suffix, and letter-shape lists alongside the interactive finders.
cluster.description: Prefix and suffix word lists (un-, re-, pre-, -ing, -ed, -tion, -ness, -able), plus double-letter, no-vowel, and all-vowel word pages.

faq.q: Do prefixes always keep their meaning?
faq.a: Mostly, but not always — "disease" doesn't mean "un-ease" in the modern sense. Treat the prefix list as a strong hint, not a rule.

faq.q: Are these lists Scrabble-legal?
faq.a: Drawn from the ENABLE1 list — close to TWL/SOWPODS but not identical. See the Scrabble hub for tournament-dictionary caveats.

tools.title: Words Starting With — Kefiw

current.description: Find every English word that starts with a given prefix. Filter by length.
current.intro: Enter a prefix and get every English word that begins with it. Useful for crossword puzzles, word games, and writing.
current.outcomeLine: Every word that starts with your chosen letters.
howTo: Type the letters you want words to start with.
Optionally restrict by length.
Results appear sorted by length, shortest first.

examples.title: Prefix: ZY
examples.body: zygote, zymase, zymurgy, zydeco…

faq.q: Is this case-sensitive?
faq.a: No. Input and results are always lowercase.

tool_logic.input_type: prefix string (a-z; non-letters and whitespace stripped, lowercased)
tool_logic.output_type: alphabetically-sorted array of every dictionary word whose initial characters match the prefix, filtered to min/max length bounds
processing_steps: Load the full English dictionary on first use via web worker — `dict.txt` (~370,105 words). (This tool does not expose the ENABLE1/full toggle; it always uses the full dictionary.)
Normalize query: trim, lowercase, strip non-letter characters.
Filter dictionary to the min/max length range.
Keep only words where `w.startsWith(prefix)`.
Sort by length ascending, then alphabetically.
Client truncates the rendered list to the first 500 and shows a "Showing first 500 of N" notice when more exist.
Debounce input by 130 ms before re-querying the worker.
data_sources: Broader English dictionary (`public/data/dict.txt`, ~370,105 entries). No ENABLE1 fallback.
tool_logic.fallback_behavior: Empty prefix returns no results. No phonetic or fuzzy-match fallback. Whitespace and punctuation in the input are silently stripped.
known_limitations: Always uses the full dictionary (dict.txt), which includes archaic, technical, and inflected forms that may not be valid Scrabble/WWF plays.
No game-list toggle — use Word Unscrambler if you need ENABLE1 output.
No proper nouns, abbreviations, or acronyms.
No pattern wildcards — prefix must be a literal string.
Rendered list is capped at 500 even when more matches exist (the full count is shown, but only 500 are displayed).
No diacritic or non-Latin-script support; non-letter characters are stripped without warning.
Default min length is 3, so 2-letter matches are hidden unless the user lowers the Min filter.

inputs: prefix (single-line text input, font-mono, autofocused, placeholder "e.g. ZY")
outputs: Result list (length-grouped, first 500 displayed)
"Showing first 500 of N" notice when results exceed 500
Summary card: total count + echoed query
Stats card: results count, shortest with length, longest with length, top length
Alphabetical-range comparison: first 5 and last 5 alphabetically (when >5 results)
Length-distribution comparison: count by length (top 4 lengths)
Takeaway: most common length and longest word
Next-step card linking to Words Ending With, Words Containing, and Word Unscrambler

Supports min and max length filters
Uses a broad English dictionary (370k+ entries)

enhanced.description: Find dictionary words that begin with a literal prefix, then narrow the results with minimum and maximum length filters.
enhanced.meta_description: Find English dictionary words starting with any prefix. Filter by length, review counts and stats, and use related tools for endings or containing letters.
enhanced.intro: Use Words Starting With when you know the first letters of a word and need a fast, spelling-based list. It helps with crossword crossings, word-game brainstorming, spelling lessons, vocabulary study, and writing prompts where a word must begin a certain way. The tool normalizes your prefix, searches a broad English dictionary in the browser, and sorts matches by length, then alphabetically. For exact game-dictionary validation, use a game-list tool instead of assuming every broad-dictionary result is playable.
enhanced.outcomeLine: A length-filtered list of dictionary words whose first letters match your prefix exactly.
howTo: Enter the starting letters you want to match, such as UN, PRE, or ZY.
Set the minimum and maximum length if your puzzle or writing task has a fixed size.
Review the summary, length groups, shortest and longest words, and the first 500 displayed results.
Switch to Words Ending With or Words Containing when the known letters are not at the beginning.

examples.expected_behavior: Returns words that start with PRE, grouped by length, so short fill and longer candidates are easier to scan.

faq.q: How do I find words starting with specific letters?
faq.a: Type the starting letters into the prefix box and adjust the length filters if needed. The tool searches for words whose first characters exactly match your input, after stripping spaces and punctuation. Use the shortest and longest result cards to decide whether your length range is too broad.

faq.q: What is the difference between a prefix and words starting with letters?
faq.a: A prefix has meaning, while starting letters may be only a spelling pattern in practical word-search and vocabulary work. UN- is often a prefix meaning not or reverse, but ST- is usually just an opening letter cluster. This tool searches spelling, so it will return both meaningful prefixes and ordinary beginnings.

faq.q: Can this tool find Scrabble-valid words starting with a prefix?
faq.a: This page uses a broad dictionary, not a guaranteed Scrabble tournament list in practical word-search and vocabulary work. Some results may be archaic, technical, inflected, or otherwise unsuitable for a specific game. For game-list filtering, use Word Finder in game-list mode or another verified game helper.

faq.q: Why are some two-letter words missing from the results?
faq.a: The default minimum length is three, so two-letter matches are hidden unless you lower it. Change the Min filter to 2 when you need short crossword fill, hooks, or compact word-game plays. The setting is designed to keep ordinary searches readable.

faq.q: Does Words Starting With support wildcards or patterns?
faq.a: No, the prefix must be a literal sequence of letters in this tool's current verified behavior. The tool does not read regex, character classes, or multi-position patterns. Use Word Finder pattern mode when you know fixed positions and need single-character wildcards.

A teacher wants examples of words beginning with un- for a spelling mini-lesson.
A writer needs alliterative options that start with ST or TR.
A word-game player wants possible rack ideas but still needs to verify game validity.
common_mistakes: Assuming every beginning is a meaningful prefix.
Using this tool for letters that appear in the middle or end of a word.
Forgetting to lower the minimum length when two-letter matches matter.
Treating broad-dictionary results as official game-list results.
Expecting punctuation or spaces to create separate search terms.
tips: Start with the shortest confirmed prefix, then add letters only when the list is too broad.
Use length filters before scanning long rare-word lists.
For prefix study, compare un-, re-, pre-, and dis- to see how meanings differ.
When the start is known but one internal letter is unknown, use Word Finder pattern mode.
Use Words Containing if the same letters can appear anywhere instead of only at the start.
limitations: Search is spelling-based, not meaning-based.
Only literal prefixes are supported; no wildcards, regex, or character classes.
The broad dictionary may include rare, archaic, technical, or inflected forms.
Proper nouns, acronyms, abbreviations, and non-Latin scripts are not included.
Only the first 500 results are displayed when the match count is larger.

enhanced.description: Find dictionary words that end with a literal suffix or final letter sequence, with length filters for tighter results.
enhanced.meta_description: Find English dictionary words ending with any suffix or final letters. Filter by length, compare result stats, and use guides for suffix patterns.
enhanced.intro: Use Words Ending With when the last letters matter: a crossword ending, a suffix lesson, a spelling pattern, or a writing line that needs a specific visual ending. The search is literal and spelling-based, so TION finds words ending in those letters, not words that sound alike. Results are filtered by length, sorted shortest first, and searched in the browser from a broad English dictionary. For sound-based rhyme work, pair this with a rhyme-specific tool rather than relying on spelling alone.
enhanced.outcomeLine: A length-filtered list of dictionary words whose final letters match your suffix exactly.
howTo: Enter the ending letters you want to match, such as ING, TION, LY, or OUS.

faq.q: How do I find words ending with a suffix?
faq.a: Type the final letters into the suffix box and adjust the length filters when setting up a practical word search. The tool returns words whose ending characters exactly match your input after normalization. Use it for spelling endings such as -ing, -ed, -ly, -tion, or any literal final sequence.

faq.q: What is the difference between a suffix and a word ending?
faq.a: A suffix usually changes meaning or grammar, while an ending can be any final letters. TION is often a suffix-like ending, but not every final letter sequence is a meaningful suffix. This tool finds spellings, so you still need context for grammar.

faq.q: Can Words Ending With find rhyming words?
faq.a: It can help brainstorm visual endings, but it does not find phonetic rhymes in practical word-search and vocabulary work. Words like rough and through share spelling patterns but sound different. Use a rhyme-focused tool when pronunciation, stress, and sound are the real goal.

faq.q: Why are two-letter ending matches not showing?
faq.a: The default minimum length is three, so two-letter words are hidden until you lower it. Set Min to 2 when your crossword, word-game hook, or short-fill search needs very short matches. The default keeps ordinary suffix searches less cluttered.

faq.q: Does the suffix tool support wildcards or regular expressions?
faq.a: No, the ending must be a literal sequence of letters in this tool's current verified behavior. The tool does not support regex, character classes, or star wildcards. Use Word Finder pattern mode when you need fixed positions with single-character wildcard slots.

faq.q: How do I find words containing a letter sequence?
faq.a: Type the exact adjacent letters you want the word to contain when setting up a practical word search. The tool searches for that sequence anywhere in each word after normalizing the input. Add length filters when you know the answer size or want fewer results.

faq.q: What does contiguous letter sequence mean?
faq.a: Contiguous means the letters appear next to each other in the same order in practical word-search and vocabulary work. Searching QUA matches aqua and quaint because Q-U-A is adjacent. It does not match words where Q, U, and A are separated across the word.

faq.q: Can this find words made from the letters I enter?
faq.a: No, this tool finds an exact substring, not an anagram or rack fit in this tool's current verified behavior. If your letters can be rearranged or do not need to stay adjacent, use Word Finder or Word Unscrambler-style tools instead. This distinction prevents misleading results.

faq.q: Why does my contains search return too many words?
faq.a: Short sequences can appear in many unrelated words, especially common pairs like IN or ER. Add another letter, tighten the length range, or switch to Words Starting With or Words Ending With if you know the position. Broader inputs naturally create broader lists.

faq.q: Does Words Containing support wildcards, regex, or letter sets?
faq.a: No, it only supports a literal adjacent substring search in this tool's current verified behavior. Wildcards, regex, character classes, and non-adjacent letter sets are outside this tool. Use Word Finder pattern mode for fixed-position blanks or Letters mode for rack-style searches.

current.description: Find words that can be made from a specific set of letters, with optional minimum and maximum length filters.
current.intro: Enter the letters you have (including blanks as ?) and filter by word length. Useful for Scrabble, Words With Friends, and general word puzzles. For prefix, suffix, or substring searches, use the dedicated Words Starting With, Words Ending With, or Words Containing tools.

tool_logic.input_type: letters (a-z plus `?` for blanks) OR a fixed-length pattern (a-z plus `?`/`_`/`.` as wildcards)
tool_logic.output_type: array of dictionary words matching either the letter rack (with min/max length filters) or the positional pattern
processing_steps: Load the chosen word list on first use: Game list = ENABLE1 or Full list = dict.txt.
If search mode is "Letters": strip whitespace, call the `unscramble` worker with minLen/maxLen — same rack-fit logic as Word Unscrambler but with separate length bounds.

If search mode is "Pattern": pattern length fixes the candidate word length. For each dictionary word of that length, call `matchesPattern` — any position whose pattern char is `?`, `_`, or `.` accepts any letter; other positions must match exactly (case-insensitive).
Letters mode sorts by length descending then alphabetically; Pattern mode returns in the source word-list order (effectively alphabetical from the dictionary file).
Optional Scrabble + WWF score display when toggled.
Lightweight variants (`5-letter-word-finder`, `6-letter-word-finder`, `7-letter-word-finder`) lock the pattern length to 5/6/7 and hide the min/max controls.
tool_logic.fallback_behavior: Empty input returns no results. No automatic min-length fallback (unlike Word Unscrambler). No phonetic fallback.
known_limitations: Pattern mode matches exact-length candidates only — a 4-character pattern cannot match a 5-letter word.
Pattern wildcards (`?`, `_`, `.`) are single-character only — no regex quantifiers, no character classes.
Letters mode does not support a "must include these letters" constraint — use Words Containing for substring matching instead.
No position-aware scoring, no premium-square modelling.
Neither word list includes proper nouns, abbreviations, or acronyms.
Does not deduplicate between the two word lists — switching mode refetches and rebuilds the index.

faq.q: How does Word Finder find words from letters?
faq.a: Letters mode checks which dictionary words can be formed from your available letters in practical word-search and vocabulary work. It respects the selected length range and can use ? as blank tiles. Results are sorted by length in Letters mode, which helps you find longer candidates first.

faq.q: What is the difference between Letters mode and Pattern mode?
faq.a: Letters mode rearranges available letters, while Pattern mode matches fixed positions in practical word-search and vocabulary work. Use Letters for racks and anagrams; use Pattern for crosswords, Wordle-style blanks, or known letter slots. Pattern wildcards are single-character placeholders, not regex symbols.

faq.q: Can I use wildcards in Word Finder?
faq.a: Yes, but the wildcard behavior depends on the mode you choose when the rule and dictionary match. In Letters mode, ? represents a blank tile from your rack. In Pattern mode, ?, _, and . each match one unknown letter. Star wildcards and regex quantifiers are not supported.

faq.q: Are Word Finder results valid for Scrabble or Words With Friends?
faq.a: Game list mode uses a compact word-game list, but board legality still depends on the game. The tool can show point values, yet it does not check board placement, hooks, premium squares, or tournament-specific dictionary changes. Treat it as a candidate finder.

faq.q: Why did Pattern mode return no words?
faq.a: Pattern mode only checks words with the exact same length as your pattern in practical word-search and vocabulary work. A four-character pattern cannot match a five-letter word, and each wildcard covers exactly one letter. Check the pattern length, fixed letters, and selected dictionary list before assuming no word exists.

faq.q: Should I use Word Finder or Words Containing?
faq.a: Use Word Finder when letters can move or positions are partly unknown for practical word study and puzzle work. Use Words Containing when you need an exact adjacent sequence inside a word. The tools answer different questions, so switching between them often solves a puzzle faster.

articles.title: What Prefixes Do in English — The Short, Useful Version
articles.subhead: How UN-, RE-, DIS-, PRE-, and friends change a word's meaning.

current.description: A plain-English guide to the most common English prefixes — meaning, origin, and worked examples.
current.outcomeLine: Know the ten most common prefixes and you can decode hundreds of words on sight.
current.intro: A prefix attaches to the front of a word and changes its meaning in a predictable way. Ten prefixes carry most of the load in everyday English — learn those and you can read new words without a dictionary.
keyPoints: UN- = not or reverse: UNHAPPY, UNLOCK, UNDO.
RE- = again or back: REDO, RETURN, REBUILD.
DIS- = not or apart: DISAGREE, DISCONNECT.
PRE- = before: PREPAY, PRESCHOOL, PREDICT.
MIS- = wrong: MISTAKE, MISSPELL, MISUNDERSTAND.
Others worth knowing: SUB-, OVER-, UNDER-, INTER-, TRANS-.

examples.title: UN- flips meaning
examples.body: HAPPY → UNHAPPY (not happy). LOCK → UNLOCK (reverse the lock).

examples.title: RE- repeats
examples.body: WRITE → REWRITE. VIEW → REVIEW (view again).

examples.title: PRE- = before
examples.body: HEAT → PREHEAT (heat beforehand). VIEW → PREVIEW.

whenToUse.note: Enter the prefix (UN, RE, DIS) and get every word that starts with it, sorted by length.
Use when you want a prefix plus another letter pattern — e.g. words starting UN- and containing ING.

faq.q: How do I know which prefix to use — UN- or DIS-?
faq.a: Usually convention, not rule. UNHAPPY but DISSATISFIED. When unsure, check a dictionary — English inherited both Germanic (UN-) and Latin (DIS-) prefixes and they rarely interchange.

faq.q: Do prefixes ever change spelling?
faq.a: Yes. IN- becomes IM- before B, M, P (IMPOSSIBLE not INPOSSIBLE), and IL-/IR- before L/R (ILLEGAL, IRREGULAR). The rule is phonetic assimilation.

enhanced.description: A practical guide to English prefixes: what they mean, how they change words, and how to use prefix searches without overreading the pattern.
enhanced.meta_description: Learn what English prefixes do, how UN-, RE-, DIS-, PRE-, MIS-, and others change meaning, and how to use prefix tools for reading and puzzles.
enhanced.intro: Prefixes help readers decode unfamiliar words, but they are clues rather than perfect rules. This guide explains the common meanings, the traps, and the best way to use prefix searches.
enhanced.outcomeLine: Learn how to use prefixes as meaning clues while checking spelling, context, and exceptions.
key_points: A prefix attaches to the beginning of a base or root and usually changes meaning.
UN-, RE-, DIS-, PRE-, and MIS- are common because they express everyday ideas: not, again, apart, before, and wrong.
Some beginnings look like prefixes but are not meaningful in the modern word.
Spelling can shift when prefixes combine with roots, especially in-, im-, il-, and ir-.
Prefix tools find spelling patterns; the reader still decides whether the prefix meaning applies.

examples.title: UN- as not or reverse
examples.body: UNHAPPY means not happy, while UNLOCK means reverse the action of locking.

examples.title: RE- as again or back
examples.body: REWRITE means write again; RETURN means come back.

examples.title: PRE- as before
examples.body: PREHEAT means heat beforehand; PREVIEW means view before the full event.

use_cases: Decoding unfamiliar vocabulary while reading.
Teaching common word parts in a classroom.
Finding examples for prefix worksheets or spelling lists.
Solving crosswords and word games where the first letters are known.
Improving writing by choosing the prefix that expresses the right shade of meaning.
common_mistakes: Assuming every word beginning with the same letters uses the same prefix meaning.
Treating UN- and DIS- as interchangeable.
Ignoring spelling assimilation such as in- changing to im- before p.

tips: Check whether removing the prefix leaves a recognizable base word.
Compare the word in a real sentence before trusting the prefix meaning.
Use the starting-with tool for spelling lists and the guide for meaning.
Study prefix pairs such as un-/dis- and pre-/ante- to learn nuance.

A spelling beginning may not be a true prefix.
The linked tools search spelling patterns, not meanings.

articles.longform_markdown:
## What a prefix actually does

A prefix is a word part placed at the beginning of a word to alter its meaning. That sounds simple, but the useful part is what it lets you do while reading, writing, or solving puzzles. When you see **unhappy**, you can often break it into **un-** plus **happy** and read it as “not happy.” When you see **rewrite**, you can read **re-** as “again” and understand the action before checking a dictionary.

The main user need is speed. A reader wants to understand a new word without stopping. A student wants to sort spelling words by pattern. A crossword solver wants to use known starting letters. A writer wants the right shade of meaning: **prepay** is not the same as **repay**, and **unlock** is not the same as **dislock**.

Prefixes are strong clues, not automatic answers. A beginning such as **un-** is often meaningful, but English also has words where the first letters are not a removable prefix in everyday use. Use the clue, then check the base word, sentence, and spelling.

## The common prefixes worth learning first

A small group of prefixes covers a large share of everyday prefix work. **UN-** often means not or reverse: **unfair**, **unhappy**, **unlock**, **undo**. **RE-** often means again or back: **reread**, **rebuild**, **return**. **DIS-** can mean not, apart, away, or opposite: **disagree**, **disconnect**, **disapprove**. **PRE-** means before: **preheat**, **preview**, **prepay**. **MIS-** means wrong or badly: **misread**, **misplace**, **misjudge**.

Other useful prefixes include **over-** for too much or above, **under-** for below or insufficient, **sub-** for under, **inter-** for between, and **trans-** for across or through. These are especially helpful in school vocabulary, science words, and news writing.

Try a simple test: remove the prefix and see whether the base still makes sense. **Misread** leaves **read**, and the full word means read wrongly. **Preview** leaves **view**, and the full word means view before. That test is not perfect, but it quickly separates many real prefixes from ordinary beginnings.

## Prefixes are clues, not guarantees

The biggest mistake is treating a prefix like a math formula. English spelling comes from Germanic, Latin, Greek, French, and other sources, so word parts do not behave with perfect consistency. **Unhappy** works, but **understand** does not mean “stand under” in ordinary modern English. **Disease** begins with letters that resemble **dis-**, but the modern meaning is not simply “not ease” in the way a beginner might expect.

Some prefixes also compete. **UN-** and **DIS-** both create negative meanings, but they are not freely interchangeable. We say **unhappy**, **dissatisfied**, **unable**, and **disagree** because convention controls which form sounds natural. A writer choosing between them should check real usage, not only the prefix definition.

Spelling can shift too. The negative prefix **in-** often changes form before certain letters: **impossible**, **illegal**, **irregular**. The meaning is still negative, but the spelling adapts to the sound of the next letter. That is why prefix learning should include examples, not only definitions.

## How to use prefix searches for puzzles and word study

When your task is spelling-based, use the [Words Starting With](/word-tools/words-starting-with/) tool. Enter **un**, **re**, **pre**, or another beginning, then narrow by length. This is useful for crossword entries, spelling lists, classroom examples, and quick vocabulary banks.

When the beginning is only one part of the clue, combine tools. If you need words that start with **un** and also contain **ing**, start with a broad prefix search, then use [Words Containing](/word-tools/words-containing/) for the internal sequence. If the puzzle has fixed positions such as **u n _ _ _ _**, use [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/) pattern mode instead of a simple prefix list.

For broader learning, compare this guide with [Common English Word Beginnings](/guides/common-english-word-beginnings/). A beginning is any first letters; a prefix is a meaningful word part. **ST-** is a common beginning in **star**, **stone**, and **street**, but it is not usually a prefix. That distinction helps you avoid overexplaining every word.

## Worked examples: UN-, RE-, and PRE-

Take **unlock**. The base is **lock**. The prefix **un-** reverses the action, so **unlock** means to open or release a lock. That is different from **unhappy**, where **un-** means not. The same prefix can have related but distinct jobs.

Take **rewrite**. The base is **write**, and **re-** means again. The word means to write again, often with changes. In **return**, the same prefix points more toward back than again. The word’s history and usage shape the final meaning.

Take **preheat**. The base is **heat**, and **pre-** means before. The word means heat before the main cooking action. In **preview**, the base is **view**, and the meaning is view before the full version. These examples show why prefixes are useful: they make new words easier to decode when the base is clear.

## Common prefix mistakes to avoid

Do not assume a word is valid just because a prefix can be attached to a base. English allows many combinations, but not every possible combination is accepted in dictionaries or word games. A spelling tool can show matches; it does not prove that a newly invented prefix form is standard.

Do not use prefix meaning alone when tone matters. **Uninterested** and **disinterested** are often confused because both look negative, but they do not always serve the same purpose. A writer should choose the word that fits the sentence, not the first word that matches the prefix.

Do not forget suffixes. Prefixes usually change meaning at the front; suffixes often change grammar at the end. If you want the full word-building picture, read [What Suffixes Do in English](/guides/what-suffixes-do-in-english/) next. Prefixes help decode meaning, but suffixes often tell you whether the word is a noun, verb, adjective, or adverb.

A good practice routine is to collect words in three columns: clear prefix examples, uncertain examples, and ordinary beginnings. Put **unhappy**, **rewrite**, and **preheat** in the clear column. Put harder words in the uncertain column until you can explain the base and meaning. Put words such as **street** or **stone** in ordinary beginnings because their first letters help spelling searches but not prefix meaning.

For a website page, this distinction also improves trust. Users should see that the tool finds letter beginnings, while the guide teaches interpretation. That keeps the [Words Starting With](/word-tools/words-starting-with/) page honest and gives the article a real teaching role instead of repeating the tool interface.

faq.q: What is a prefix in English?
faq.a: A prefix is a word part added to the beginning of a word to change meaning. Common examples include un-, re-, pre-, dis-, and mis-. Prefixes help readers decode unfamiliar vocabulary, but the surrounding word and sentence still matter.

faq.q: How do prefixes change the meaning of words?
faq.a: Prefixes usually add ideas such as not, again, before, under, across, or wrong in practical word-search and vocabulary work. For example, re- can mean again in rewrite, while pre- can mean before in preview. Some prefixes have more than one related meaning, so examples are essential.

faq.q: What is the difference between a prefix and a word beginning?
faq.a: A prefix is meaningful, while a word beginning may be only a spelling pattern for practical word study and puzzle work. UN- in unhappy changes meaning, but ST- in stone is just an initial cluster. Prefix search tools find beginnings, so users must judge meaning separately.

faq.q: Can any prefix be added to any word?
faq.a: No, English does not accept every possible prefix-and-base combination as standard in this tool's current verified behavior. Some combinations sound natural, some are rare, and some are not dictionary words. When writing or playing word games, check the actual word rather than building forms mechanically.

faq.q: Why do prefixes sometimes change spelling?
faq.a: Some prefixes change spelling to match the sound of the following letter in practical word-search and vocabulary work. The negative prefix in- becomes im- in impossible, il- in illegal, and ir- in irregular. These changes are easier to learn through grouped examples than abstract rules.

faq.q: Should I use a prefix tool for Scrabble words?
faq.a: Use a prefix tool for brainstorming, but verify game validity separately when choosing the correct search workflow. A broad starting-with list may include words outside a specific game dictionary. If the exact game list matters, use a verified game-list mode or game helper before playing.

articles.title: What Suffixes Do in English — Changing Part of Speech
articles.subhead: How -ING, -ED, -LY, -TION, and friends turn one word into another.

current.description: Plain-English guide to the most common English suffixes — how they change word class and meaning, with examples.
current.outcomeLine: Suffixes mostly change the part of speech — verb to noun, noun to adjective, adjective to adverb.
current.intro: A suffix attaches to the end of a word and usually changes its part of speech rather than its core meaning. Learn the common suffix patterns and you can turn any noun into an adjective or any verb into a noun.
keyPoints: -ING and -ED form verb tenses: RUN → RUNNING, RUNNED (wait — RAN, irregular). WALK → WALKING, WALKED.
-TION / -SION turn verbs into nouns: ACT → ACTION, DECIDE → DECISION.
-LY turns adjectives into adverbs: QUICK → QUICKLY, HAPPY → HAPPILY.
-NESS and -ITY turn adjectives into nouns: DARK → DARKNESS, ABLE → ABILITY.
-FUL and -LESS turn nouns into adjectives: HOPE → HOPEFUL / HOPELESS.

examples.title: Verb → noun
examples.body: ACT → ACTION. DECIDE → DECISION. ENJOY → ENJOYMENT. Common suffixes: -TION, -SION, -MENT, -ANCE.

examples.title: Adjective → adverb
examples.body: QUICK → QUICKLY. HAPPY → HAPPILY (Y becomes I). FULL → FULLY. Drop the double-L when adding -LY.

examples.title: Noun → adjective
examples.body: HOPE → HOPEFUL (full of hope) / HOPELESS (without hope). Same root, opposite meaning via suffix.

whenToUse.note: Enter the suffix (ING, TION, LY) and get every word ending in it.
For suffix-plus-pattern searches (words ending -ING and containing TR).

faq.q: Why does Y change to I before some suffixes?
faq.a: When adding -LY, -ED, -ES, -EST to a word ending in consonant+Y: HAPPY → HAPPILY, CRY → CRIED. Vowel+Y words keep the Y: PLAY → PLAYED.

faq.q: Do suffixes always change part of speech?
faq.a: Usually yes, but not always. -HOOD keeps nouns as nouns (CHILD → CHILDHOOD). The general rule is "suffix changes class," with specific exceptions.

articles.longform_markdown:
## What a suffix does

A suffix is a word part added to the end of a word. Prefixes often change meaning from the front; suffixes often change the word’s grammatical job from the back. A verb can become a noun, an adjective can become an adverb, and a base word can become past tense, plural, comparative, or abstract.

That is why users search suffixes for different reasons. A student may be learning why **happy** becomes **happiness**. A writer may be checking whether **quickly** or **quickness** fits a sentence. A crossword solver may know the answer ends in **-tion**. A word-game player may want to spot extensions like **-ed**, **-er**, or **-ing**.

The key is to ask two questions: what letters are at the end, and what job do those letters perform? The [Words Ending With](/word-tools/words-ending-with/) tool answers the spelling question. This guide answers the meaning and grammar question.

## Common suffix jobs

Some suffixes mark verb forms. **-ING** forms present participles and gerunds: **walking**, **thinking**, **building**. **-ED** marks regular past tense or past participle forms: **walked**, **helped**, **opened**. Irregular verbs break that pattern, which is why **run** becomes **ran** in the past tense rather than **runned**.

Other suffixes turn verbs into nouns. **-TION** and **-SION** are common in formal words: **act** becomes **action**, **decide** becomes **decision**. **-MENT** also creates nouns: **enjoyment**, **agreement**, **movement**. **-NESS** turns adjectives into nouns: **darkness**, **kindness**, **readiness**.

Adjective and adverb suffixes are just as useful. **-FUL** means full of, as in **hopeful**. **-LESS** means without, as in **hopeless**. **-ABLE** and **-IBLE** often mean capable of or fit for, as in **readable** and **visible**. **-LY** often turns adjectives into adverbs: **quick** becomes **quickly**.

## Spelling changes before suffixes

Suffixes are not always added by simply attaching letters. English often changes the base word first. A final silent **e** may drop before a vowel-starting suffix: **make** becomes **making**, and **write** becomes **writing**. But the **e** may stay before a consonant-starting suffix: **hope** becomes **hopeful**.

Words ending in consonant plus **y** often change **y** to **i** before certain suffixes: **happy** becomes **happily**, **cry** becomes **cried**, and **beauty** becomes **beautiful**. Words ending in vowel plus **y** usually keep the **y**: **play** becomes **played**.

Short stressed words may double a final consonant before **-ing** or **-ed**: **run** becomes **running**, **stop** becomes **stopped**. This is a spelling pattern, not a universal rule. The safest learning method is to group examples and notice what changes.

## Suffixes for puzzles and word games

Suffix knowledge is practical in puzzles because endings narrow possibilities quickly. If a crossword answer ends in **-tion**, the word is likely a noun and often has a formal or abstract meaning. If a clue asks for an adverb and the answer ends in **-ly**, that can confirm the grammar. If a game board lets you extend a word with **-ed**, **-er**, or **-s**, a small ending can create a stronger play.

Use the [Words Ending With](/word-tools/words-ending-with/) tool when the final letters are confirmed. Type **tion**, **ing**, **ly**, or **ness**, then set the answer length. Use [Words Containing](/word-tools/words-containing/) when the ending is not fixed but a cluster appears somewhere inside the word. Use [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/) pattern mode when the entire answer length and several positions are known.

For deeper pattern study, read [Common English Word Endings](/guides/common-english-word-endings/). That page focuses on high-frequency endings and how they help search strategy. This page focuses on what suffixes do grammatically.

## Worked example: from base word to word family

Start with the verb **act**. Add **-ion** and the word becomes **action**, a noun. Add **-ive** and it becomes **active**, an adjective. Add **-ly** to the adjective and it becomes **actively**, an adverb. The root idea stays connected, but each suffix changes how the word can be used in a sentence.

Now try **hope**. Add **-ful** and you get **hopeful**, meaning full of hope. Add **-less** and you get **hopeless**, meaning without hope. Add **-ness** to **hopeful** and you get **hopefulness**, an abstract noun. The suffixes do more than add letters; they organize meaning into usable grammar.

This is why suffixes are powerful for writing. If a sentence needs a noun, **decision** may fit better than **decide**. If it needs an adverb, **carefully** may fit better than **careful**. Suffixes give you related words with different sentence roles.

## Common suffix mistakes

Do not assume every final letter sequence is a suffix. **Thing** ends in **ing**, but it is not a verb form built from **th**. A spelling search for **ing** will find it, but grammar still requires judgment.

Do not assume words with the same ending sound alike. **Though**, **through**, and **rough** show why spelling endings are not enough for rhyme. A suffix tool can help with spelling patterns, but pronunciation needs separate attention.

Do not ignore prefixes. Many real words use both a prefix and a suffix: **unhappiness**, **misjudgment**, **preheating**. Prefixes usually alter meaning at the front, while suffixes often shape grammar at the end. Read [What Prefixes Do in English](/guides/what-prefixes-do-in-english/) to connect the two halves of word formation.

A useful classroom or self-study routine is to start with one base word and build a family. Try **care**, **careful**, **carefully**, **careless**, and **carelessness**. The spelling changes are manageable, and the grammar changes are visible. Then repeat the process with a verb such as **decide**: **decision**, **decisive**, **decisively**. This trains users to see suffixes as part of a system.

For puzzle work, the same routine helps with clue grammar. If a clue asks for an abstract noun, endings such as **-ness**, **-ity**, **-ment**, and **-tion** become more likely. If it asks for manner, **-ly** may be useful. The ending does not solve the clue by itself, but it gives the solver a better first filter.

A final useful habit is to read the word aloud after adding a suffix. Spelling rules are visual, but pronunciation often explains why a form feels natural or awkward. Then check the sentence role. If the new word names a thing, it is likely acting as a noun. If it describes how something happens, it may be an adverb. That quick grammar check turns suffix study into better writing, not just better spelling.

faq.q: What is a suffix in English?
faq.a: A suffix is a word part added to the end of a word to change grammar or meaning. Common suffixes include -ing, -ed, -ly, -tion, -ness, -ful, and -able. Many suffixes change part of speech.

faq.q: How do suffixes change a word?
faq.a: Suffixes can mark tense, create nouns, form adjectives, make adverbs, or show comparison in practical word-search and vocabulary work. Walk becomes walking or walked, quick becomes quickly, and kind becomes kindness. The base idea often remains, but the sentence role changes.

faq.q: What is the difference between a suffix and a word ending?
faq.a: A suffix has a grammatical or meaning job, while an ending may be only final letters. The letters ing can be a suffix in walking, but not every word ending in ing is built the same way.

faq.q: Why does spelling change before some suffixes?
faq.a: Spelling changes keep pronunciation or spelling patterns natural when suffixes are added for practical word study and puzzle work. Final e may drop before -ing, y may change to i, and consonants may double in short stressed words. Examples are easier to learn than isolated rules.

faq.q: Can suffixes help with crossword solving?
faq.a: Yes, suffixes can reveal both ending letters and likely grammar in crossword clues when the rule and dictionary match. An answer ending in -tion is often a noun, while -ly often points to an adverb. Use length and crossings to avoid overguessing.

faq.q: Are all words ending in -ed past tense verbs?
faq.a: No, some -ed words are adjectives, names, or historical forms rather than simple past tense verbs. A spelling search will find final letters, but the clue or sentence decides the grammar. Check whether the word names an action, describes a quality, or functions as something else.

articles.longform_markdown:
## Why endings are powerful search clues

The end of a word often carries useful information. In grammar, it may show tense, number, part of speech, or word family. In puzzles, it can shrink a huge candidate set into a short list. In word games, it can turn a small base into a longer play. That is why users search common endings so often: they are trying to move from a vague clue to a manageable set of words.

A crossword solver who knows an answer ends in **-tion** can focus on noun-like candidates. A student studying spelling may compare **-ing**, **-ed**, and **-er**. A writer may notice too many **-ly** adverbs and look for stronger verbs. A word-game player may watch for places to add **-s**, **-ed**, or **-ing** to an existing stem.

The [Words Ending With](/word-tools/words-ending-with/) tool handles the literal search. This guide helps decide which endings are worth searching and what they usually tell you.

## The high-frequency endings to know

Some endings show up constantly because they attach to many everyday words. **-S** and **-ES** mark plurals and some verb forms. **-ED** marks regular past tense and past participles. **-ING** forms present participles and gerunds. **-ER** can mark a person, a comparative adjective, or a word-game-friendly ending. **-LY** often marks adverbs.

Longer endings help with formal vocabulary. **-TION** and **-SION** often create nouns from verbs: **action**, **decision**, **expansion**. **-MENT** appears in **agreement**, **movement**, and **judgment**. **-NESS** creates abstract nouns such as **kindness** and **darkness**. **-ABLE** and **-IBLE** create adjectives such as **readable** and **visible**.

These endings are useful because they are productive. You can learn one pattern and recognize many words. But productivity is not the same as permission to invent any form. Always check the actual word when correctness matters.

## Endings are not always suffixes

A word ending is any final letter sequence. A suffix is a meaningful word part. The difference matters. **Walking** ends in **-ing** and uses **-ing** as a suffix. **Thing** also ends in **ing**, but the final letters are not the same grammatical suffix in normal modern use. A search tool will find both because it searches spelling, not grammar.

The same issue appears with **-er**. In **teacher**, **-er** marks a person who teaches. In **smaller**, **-er** marks comparison. In **river**, the final letters are not a simple suffix you can remove to get a modern base word **riv**. The spelling is the same, but the analysis differs.

This is why [What Suffixes Do in English](/guides/what-suffixes-do-in-english/) is a useful companion. It explains the grammatical job of suffixes, while this page focuses on searchable endings and practical patterns.

## How to use endings in crosswords and word games

For crosswords, length is everything. Do not search **tion** with the default range if the clue answer has eight boxes. Set the minimum and maximum to 8 so the list matches the grid. If you know an internal crossing too, move to [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/) pattern mode and enter the exact slots.

For word games, endings help you think about extensions. A base word may take **-s**, **-ed**, **-er**, or **-ing**, but game validity depends on the dictionary and board. The ending may be legal in ordinary English but not useful on the current board, or the resulting form may not be accepted in the selected game list. Treat endings as candidate ideas, then verify.

For writing, endings can reveal style habits. A paragraph overloaded with **-ly** adverbs may feel weaker than one built with precise verbs. A dense run of **-tion** nouns may sound abstract or formal. Endings are not bad, but noticing them helps revise with intention.

## Worked ending searches

Suppose a crossword clue is “formal request” and the answer has eight letters ending in **-tion**. Enter **tion** in [Words Ending With](/word-tools/words-ending-with/) and set both length filters to 8. The list is now much easier to scan than every word ending in **tion**.

Suppose a spelling lesson is about **-able** and **-ible**. Search each ending separately and compare examples. Students can notice that the endings sound similar, but spelling depends on word history and convention. A list helps, but repeated examples build memory.

Suppose you have a word-game rack with many vowels and want longer candidates. Endings such as **-ing**, **-er**, and **-ed** can give structure to the rack. Use [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/) for the actual letters, then compare the endings that appear in the results.

## Mistakes that make ending searches noisy

The shortest endings create the most noise. Searching **e** or **s** can return huge lists, many of them irrelevant. Use longer endings when possible, or combine a short ending with exact length. Searching **tion**, **ness**, or **able** is usually more informative than searching one letter.

Do not assume spelling equals sound. **Though**, **rough**, and **through** show why ending letters can mislead poets and lyric writers. For visual pattern work, spelling endings are fine. For rhyme, pronunciation matters.

Do not rely on one ending clue when the task requires meaning. If a clue asks for a person, **-er** may help, but not every **-er** word names a person. Use clue meaning, crossings, and length together. The best ending searches support judgment; they do not replace it.

## What to learn after common endings

Once you know common endings, learn common beginnings. Prefixes such as **un-**, **re-**, and **pre-** work from the other side of the word and often change meaning before the ending changes grammar. The [Common English Word Beginnings](/guides/common-english-word-beginnings/) guide helps connect the front of the word to the ending patterns here.

When you have a full pattern rather than just an ending, use [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/). When you only know that a sequence appears somewhere, use [Words Containing](/word-tools/words-containing/). Moving between these tools is the practical skill: choose the search that matches what you actually know.

For content pages, endings also make strong internal-link anchors because they naturally connect tools and lessons. A user reading about **-tion** should be able to move to [Words Ending With](/word-tools/words-ending-with/) for examples, then to [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/) when a fixed pattern appears. That path mirrors the user’s real workflow: learn the pattern, search the ending, then solve the specific puzzle.

When building word lists, avoid presenting endings as magic shortcuts. Instead, show the search logic. First define the ending, then set length, then use clue meaning, then verify the word. This keeps the page helpful for beginners without overstating what a spelling pattern can prove.

faq.q: What are the most common English word endings?
faq.a: Common English word endings include -s, -ed, -ing, -er, -ly, -tion, -ness, and -able in practical word-search and vocabulary work. The most useful ending depends on the task: spelling, grammar, crossword solving, writing style, or word-game play.

faq.q: How do word endings help with crossword puzzles?
faq.a: Word endings narrow crossword candidates by final letters, likely grammar, and answer length in practical word-search and vocabulary work. An answer ending in -tion is often a noun, while -ly may signal an adverb. Combine endings with crossings instead of guessing from endings alone.

faq.q: What is the difference between a word ending and a suffix?
faq.a: A suffix has meaning or grammar, while a word ending is just final spelling for practical word study and puzzle work. Walking uses -ing as a suffix, but thing merely ends with the same letters. Search tools find spellings; grammar requires interpretation.

faq.q: Can common endings help in Scrabble or word games?
faq.a: Yes, common endings can suggest extensions and longer candidates in word games when the rule and dictionary match. Endings like -s, -ed, -er, and -ing often matter, but board placement and the selected game dictionary decide whether a play is valid and valuable.

faq.q: Why does my ending search show too many results?
faq.a: Very short endings match too many words unless you also filter by length in practical word-search and vocabulary work. Add more ending letters, set an exact answer length, or switch to pattern search when internal letters are known. Broad endings naturally create noisy lists.

faq.q: Should I use endings to find rhymes?
faq.a: Use endings for visual rhyme brainstorming, not final rhyme decisions when choosing the correct search workflow. English spelling and pronunciation often diverge, so words with the same final letters may not rhyme. Check sound, stress, and context before choosing a poem or lyric word.

articles.longform_markdown:
## Why beginnings matter

The first letters of a word carry a lot of search power. In a crossword, a confirmed beginning can eliminate most candidates. In vocabulary study, a beginning may reveal a prefix such as **un-**, **re-**, or **pre-**. In spelling, initial clusters such as **str-**, **ch-**, and **th-** help learners recognize familiar patterns. In writing, beginnings help create alliteration and themed word banks.

The user’s real task is usually not “learn a list.” It is to find the right word faster. A solver may know that a five-letter answer starts with **tr**. A teacher may need examples of **pre-** words. A player may want a word that fits a board opening. The [Words Starting With](/word-tools/words-starting-with/) tool turns that starting information into a filtered list.

The important distinction is that a beginning is not always a prefix. Every prefix is a beginning, but many beginnings are just spelling patterns.

## Prefix beginnings and ordinary beginnings

A prefix has meaning. **UN-** often means not or reverse, as in **unfair** and **unlock**. **RE-** often means again or back, as in **rewrite** and **return**. **PRE-** means before, as in **preheat** and **preview**. **DIS-** can mean not, apart, or opposite, as in **disagree** and **disconnect**. These beginnings help decode meaning.

An ordinary beginning may be common without carrying a separate meaning. **ST-** begins **star**, **stone**, **street**, and **strong**. **CH-** begins **chair**, **chase**, and **choice**. **TR-** begins **tree**, **track**, and **train**. These clusters are useful for searching and spelling, but you usually cannot remove them as prefixes.

This matters because overexplaining can mislead learners. A student should not be taught that **st-** means something just because many words begin that way. Use [What Prefixes Do in English](/guides/what-prefixes-do-in-english/) when meaning is the goal, and use beginning searches when spelling position is the goal.

## Common beginnings for fast searches

For prefix study, start with **un-**, **re-**, **pre-**, **dis-**, **mis-**, **over-**, and **under-**. These beginnings appear in everyday words and create understandable families. You can compare **unhappy**, **redo**, **prepay**, **disagree**, **misread**, **overcook**, and **underpay** to see how each prefix shifts meaning.

For spelling and puzzle work, common initial clusters include **st-**, **tr-**, **str-**, **ch-**, **sh-**, **th-**, **cl-**, **br-**, **gr-**, and **pr-**. These are useful because they narrow a list without requiring the beginning to be meaningful. If a crossword answer starts with **str**, search **str** and set the answer length.

For rare starts, be cautious. Beginnings such as **zy-**, **ps-**, **pt-**, **mn-**, and **kn-** may point to borrowed, technical, archaic, or specialized words. They are not automatic no-result signals, but they do tell you to expect a smaller and stranger list.

## How to search by beginning without wasting time

Start with the confirmed letters, not a guess. If a crossword gives **pr**, search **pr** first. If the list is too broad, add a third letter or exact length. Searching too many guessed letters can hide the correct answer. Searching too few letters can create a list that is too long to scan.

Set length early. If you know the answer has seven letters, set min and max to 7. This turns a general beginning list into a puzzle-specific candidate set. The same habit helps teachers preparing examples: a short list of four- to six-letter words may be more useful for beginners than a huge full-range list.

Use [Words Containing](/word-tools/words-containing/) when the letter sequence is not at the beginning. Use [Words Ending With](/word-tools/words-ending-with/) when the final letters matter. Use [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/) when you have a fixed pattern, blank positions, or rack letters that can move.

## Worked examples

Imagine a clue answer has six letters and begins **un**. A raw search for **un** may produce many results, because **un-** is productive and also appears in words where the letters do not act the same way. Set the length to six and scan the candidates. Then use the clue meaning to decide whether the word is a negative, a reversal, or simply a word that starts with those letters.

Now imagine a spelling lesson on **str-**. This is not usually a prefix lesson. It is a consonant-cluster lesson. Search **str**, choose a manageable length range, and group examples by sound or difficulty. Students can compare **strong**, **street**, **string**, and **strike** without needing a prefix definition.

For writing, suppose you want a product name or poem line with a **br** sound. A beginning search can create a word bank. But the final choice still depends on tone, meaning, and rhythm. The tool supplies candidates; the writer supplies judgment.

## Mistakes with word beginnings

Do not treat the most common beginnings as the best word choices. A common start may produce familiar words, but the best answer depends on the clue or sentence. Search results are inputs for thinking, not final decisions.

Do not assume rare starts are impossible. English contains borrowed and technical words that begin with unusual clusters. If you are solving a hard crossword or quiz, rare beginnings may be exactly what the puzzle wants.

Do not use beginning search when you really need a prefix meaning. The [Words Starting With](/word-tools/words-starting-with/) tool can show everything beginning **pre**, but it does not decide whether **pre-** means before in each word. For meaning, use examples and context. For spelling position, use the tool.

## What to learn next

Beginnings work best when paired with endings. A word may begin with a prefix and end with a suffix: **unhappiness**, **rebuilding**, **prepayment**. Read [Common English Word Endings](/guides/common-english-word-endings/) and [What Suffixes Do in English](/guides/what-suffixes-do-in-english/) to see how the back of the word changes grammar.

For tool workflow, keep the search question exact. “What starts with these letters?” goes to starting search. “What ends with these letters?” goes to ending search. “What contains this sequence?” goes to containing search. “What fits this whole pattern?” goes to Word Finder. That choice is what makes the cluster useful.

For study, it helps to keep two notebooks or columns: meaningful beginnings and useful clusters. Put **un-**, **re-**, **pre-**, **mis-**, and **dis-** in the meaningful column. Put **st-**, **str-**, **ch-**, **sh-**, and **tr-** in the cluster column. Both columns are useful, but they answer different questions.

This separation also helps users choose tools. If the question is “what words start with these letters,” the starting tool is enough. If the question is “what does this beginning mean,” a prefix guide is needed. If the question is “what full pattern fits my puzzle,” [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/) is usually the better next step.

faq.q: What are common English word beginnings?
faq.a: Common English word beginnings include meaningful prefixes and frequent initial clusters in practical word-search and vocabulary work. Examples include un-, re-, pre-, dis-, st-, ch-, tr-, and str-. Some beginnings change meaning, while others simply help with spelling or puzzle searching.

faq.q: What is the difference between a beginning and a prefix?
faq.a: A beginning is any first letter sequence, while a prefix is a meaningful word part. UN- in unhappy is a prefix, but ST- in stone is usually only a beginning. Search tools find both types.

faq.q: How do word beginnings help with crosswords?
faq.a: Known beginning letters reduce crossword candidates before you even use the clue meaning in practical word-search and vocabulary work. Add exact length and crossings for better results. A three-letter start plus answer length can turn a large dictionary into a short candidate list.

faq.q: Can rare beginnings still make valid English words?
faq.a: Yes, rare beginnings can appear in borrowed, technical, archaic, or specialized words when the rule and dictionary match. A rare start often means fewer matches, not zero matches. Hard puzzles sometimes use rare starts precisely because they are distinctive.

faq.q: Why does a prefix search include words that are not prefix examples?
faq.a: A starting-with search matches spelling, not meaning in practical word-search and vocabulary work for learners and solvers. If you search re, the tool returns words beginning with those letters, even when re- is not acting as a modern prefix. Use context and the prefix guide for meaning.

faq.q: Should I use common beginnings for word games?
faq.a: Common beginnings are useful for brainstorming, but they do not prove a game play is legal. Use them to spot candidates, then verify the word in the right game list and check whether it fits the board.

articles.longform_markdown:
## Why UN words are useful

Words starting with **un** are useful because they sit at the intersection of meaning and search. In many everyday words, **un-** creates a negative or reversal: **unhappy** means not happy, while **unlock** means reverse the action of locking. That makes UN words valuable for vocabulary study, spelling lessons, writing contrast, and puzzle solving.

A user landing on a UN word list is usually trying to do something specific. A student may need prefix examples. A crossword solver may know the first two letters. A writer may want a word that expresses absence, reversal, or contrast. A word-game player may be checking whether a rack can make a useful word that starts with **un**.

Use this page as a focused reference, then move to the right tool for the next constraint. For a live prefix search, use [Words Starting With](/word-tools/words-starting-with/). For movable rack letters or fixed pattern slots, use [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/).

## What UN usually means

The prefix **un-** often has two related jobs. First, it can mean **not**, especially with adjectives and participles: **unfair**, **unable**, **unclear**, **unfinished**. Second, it can mean **reverse** or **remove**, especially with verbs: **unlock**, **untie**, **unpack**, **undo**.

These meanings are close but not identical. **Unhappy** means not happy. **Unlock** does not mean “not lock” in the same way; it means reverse the locking action. **Uncover** can mean remove a cover or reveal something. The exact meaning depends on the base word and sentence.

This is why UN is such a productive prefix in English. It gives speakers a compact way to create opposites and reversals. Still, not every possible **un-** combination is standard. A writer may understand an invented form, but a dictionary, editor, or game list may not accept it.

## UN words in crosswords and word games

In crosswords, **un** is a strong beginning because it combines common letters with a likely meaning. If a clue suggests “not,” “opposite,” “reverse,” or “remove,” an UN word may be worth testing. Add the exact answer length before scanning. A six-letter UN answer and a ten-letter UN answer are very different search tasks.

For word games, UN can help form longer words, but the board matters. A word that looks playable in isolation may not fit the available anchors. A high-value UN word may be less useful than a shorter word placed on a premium square. If you need candidate generation from a rack, use [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/) and then verify the actual board play.

The safest wording for this page is reference, not guarantee. Use the list to discover candidates and patterns. Use the game’s accepted dictionary or a verified game-list mode when a specific play matters.

## UN vs DIS and other negative prefixes

UN and DIS can both create negative meanings, but they are not interchangeable. English uses **unhappy**, **unable**, and **uncertain**, but **dissatisfied**, **disagree**, and **disconnect**. A native speaker usually learns these as conventions, not formulas.

The difference is partly history and partly usage. **UN-** comes from a Germanic pattern and is extremely common with adjectives and reversive verbs. **DIS-** comes through Latin and French patterns and often suggests separation, opposition, or undoing in a different way. That does not mean one rule predicts every word.

For a broader explanation, read [What Prefixes Do in English](/guides/what-prefixes-do-in-english/). For a list of other common starts, use [Common English Word Beginnings](/guides/common-english-word-beginnings/).

## Writing with UN words

UN words can sound direct and efficient. **Unclear** is shorter than “not clear.” **Unfair** is sharper than “not fair.” **Unfinished** immediately tells the reader that a task is incomplete. In writing, that compactness is useful, especially when contrast matters.

But UN words can also feel blunt. **Unkind** may sound softer than **cruel**, while **untrue** may sound less direct than **false**. The best choice depends on tone. If you are writing a poem, story, article, or headline, use the UN list for options, then choose the word that fits meaning, rhythm, and voice.

Avoid stacking too many UN words in one sentence unless repetition is intentional. “The unfinished, unclear, unfair plan” may be accurate, but it can also sound heavy. A list helps you find choices; revision helps you pick the right one.

## Common mistakes with UN words

Do not add UN to any word and assume the result is standard. Some combinations are common, some are rare, and some sound invented. That matters for formal writing and word games.

Do not assume every word beginning with **un** is transparent. Some words have histories or forms that are not obvious from modern prefix rules. Search tools match letters; they do not explain every word’s origin.

Do not use the UN list when the letters could appear elsewhere. If the known letters are in the middle, use [Words Containing](/word-tools/words-containing/). If you know the ending instead, use [Words Ending With](/word-tools/words-ending-with/). Choosing the right search saves time.

## A practical UN-word workflow

For vocabulary, start with a small set: **unfair**, **unhappy**, **unclear**, **unlock**, **undo**, **untie**. Sort them into “not” and “reverse” meanings. Then add longer examples and ask whether the base word is still clear.

For crosswords, search **un** with the exact length. Read the clue after scanning the likely candidates, not before making a wild guess. If the pattern has fixed letters after UN, switch to Word Finder pattern mode.

For word games, use the list to study patterns, but use the actual rack and board before choosing. A good player learns word families, then applies them to the specific position. UN words are a useful family because they appear often, carry meaning, and create many practical candidates.

For practice, make two UN columns: “not” and “reverse.” Put **unfair**, **unclear**, and **unable** under not. Put **unlock**, **untie**, and **unpack** under reverse. Then add harder examples and ask which column they fit. If the word does not fit either column cleanly, mark it as a spelling match that needs context.

This simple sorting exercise turns a word list into a learning page. It helps students understand the prefix, helps writers choose better words, and helps solvers notice why a clue may be pointing toward an UN answer. The list is no longer just a set of words; it becomes a set of decisions.

faq.a: The prefix un- usually means not, reverse, remove, or undo an action in common modern English usage and examples. It appears in words such as unhappy, unfair, unlock, untie, and undo. The exact meaning depends on the base word and sentence.

faq.q: How do I find words starting with UN by length?
faq.a: Use a starting-with search for UN and set the exact length range when choosing the correct search workflow. This is especially helpful for crosswords, spelling lists, and word-game practice. If later letters are fixed, use Word Finder pattern mode instead.

faq.q: What is the difference between un- and dis-?
faq.a: UN- and DIS- both create negative meanings, but they are not freely interchangeable for practical word study and puzzle work. English says unhappy and dissatisfied, unable and disagree. Usage history decides many forms, so check real words rather than swapping prefixes mechanically.

faq.q: Can I add un- to any English word?
faq.a: No, not every un- combination is accepted as a standard word in this tool's current verified behavior. Some invented forms are understandable but unusual, while others are not accepted in dictionaries or games. Check the exact word when correctness matters.

faq.q: Are UN words valid in Scrabble or word games?
faq.a: Some UN words are playable, but validity depends on the game dictionary for practical word study and puzzle work. Use the page for discovery and practice, then verify a specific play in a game-list tool or the game’s accepted dictionary before relying on it.

faq.q: Why does a UN search include words that do not feel negative?
faq.a: A starts-with search matches letters, not meaning or word history in practical word-search and vocabulary work. Some words beginning with un may not behave like simple un- prefix examples. Use the prefix guide and context when the meaning matters.

articles.longform_markdown:
## Why RE words matter

Words starting with **re** are common because English often needs a compact way to express repetition, return, repair, revision, and response. **Rewrite** means write again. **Rebuild** means build again. **Return** means come back. These meanings make RE words useful for students, editors, crossword solvers, and word-game players.

A user searching RE words may be trying to solve a clue like “do again,” write about revision, build a spelling list, or find a word-game candidate from a rack. The word list gives a focused family, but the next step depends on the task. Use [Words Starting With](/word-tools/words-starting-with/) when you need live length filters, and use [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/) when your available letters or fixed positions matter.

RE is productive, but it is not a license to create any word. Some re- forms are standard, some are hyphenated in ordinary prose, and some are not accepted in formal writing or game lists.

## What RE usually means

The most familiar meaning of **re-** is **again**. You can **redo** a task, **rewrite** a paragraph, **repaint** a wall, **reheat** food, or **restart** a device. The prefix tells the reader that an action happens another time.

RE can also mean **back**. **Return** means come back. **Recall** can mean call back to mind. **Recede** means move back. In these words, the idea is not always simple repetition; it may be movement or reference backward.

Some words begin with **re** without feeling like transparent modern prefix examples. A spelling search does not decide etymology. It simply shows words beginning with those letters. When meaning matters, read the word in context and compare it with the base if there is a clear one.

## RE words in writing and revision

RE words are natural in editing and process writing. **Revise**, **rewrite**, **review**, **rework**, **reorder**, and **rephrase** all describe useful steps. They help writers talk about improvement without long explanations. A teacher can use RE words to show that writing is not one action; it is a cycle.

Tone matters. **Rewrite** is direct. **Revise** may sound more formal. **Rework** suggests larger changes. **Retell** fits stories and classroom work. A writer should choose the RE word that matches the action, not merely the first word in the list.

Too many RE words can feel repetitive because the prefix itself repeats. If a paragraph says “review, revise, rewrite, and resubmit,” that may be intentional in an instruction list. In prose, vary the language when the repetition is not the point.

## RE words in crosswords and word games

Crossword clues often use “again,” “back,” “over,” or “return” to point toward RE words. If the answer starts with RE, set the exact length before scanning. A four-letter RE answer and an eight-letter RE answer live in different candidate worlds.

In word games, RE is useful because it begins many short and medium words. But a candidate is only useful if it fits the board and the game dictionary. A broad word list can teach possibilities; it cannot model premium squares, hooks, or parallel plays. Use a game-aware helper or dictionary when a play matters.

If the letters after RE are known, [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/) pattern mode is better than a simple list. For example, a pattern like **re??d** asks a more precise question than “all RE words.”

## RE vs UN and PRE

RE, UN, and PRE are all common beginnings, but they do different jobs. **UN-** often negates or reverses: **unfair**, **unlock**. **RE-** often repeats or returns: **redo**, **return**. **PRE-** points before: **prepay**, **preview**.

The three prefixes can appear near the same base ideas but create different meanings. **Pay**, **repay**, and **prepay** are not interchangeable. **Do**, **redo**, and **undo** point in different directions. These contrasts are excellent for classroom examples because learners can feel how small beginnings change meaning.

For a broader prefix explanation, read [What Prefixes Do in English](/guides/what-prefixes-do-in-english/). For other high-frequency starts, use [Common English Word Beginnings](/guides/common-english-word-beginnings/).

## Common mistakes with RE words

Do not assume every RE word means again. **Real**, **reason**, and other RE-starting words may not be useful prefix examples for a beginner lesson. A starts-with list finds spelling; it does not label meaning.

Do not hyphenate automatically. Many RE words are written solid, especially common ones such as **redo**, **rewrite**, and **rebuild**. Hyphens may appear in some style choices or to avoid ambiguity, but hyphenated forms are usually not what word-game lists accept.

Do not invent RE forms for formal writing without checking. Modern English accepts many new re- combinations, especially in technology and workplace writing, but acceptance varies. A readable invented form may be fine in a casual sentence and wrong in a strict game.

## A practical RE workflow

For prefix learning, sort RE words by meaning: again, back, response, or unclear. Put **rewrite**, **replay**, and **reheat** in the “again” group. Put **return**, **retreat**, and **recall** in the “back” group. Discuss any words that do not fit easily.

For crosswords, search **re** with exact length and then use crossings. For rack letters, use Word Finder rather than browsing an RE list manually. For writing, use the list to find the action you mean, then check whether a stronger non-RE word would sound more natural.

When the task changes, change tools. Use [Words Containing](/word-tools/words-containing/) for internal sequences, [Words Ending With](/word-tools/words-ending-with/) for suffix-like endings, and [Words Starting With](/word-tools/words-starting-with/) for live RE searches.

A strong way to study RE words is to sort them by task. Revision words include **revise**, **rewrite**, **review**, and **rework**. Return words include **return**, **retreat**, and **recall**. Repeat-action words include **redo**, **replay**, and **restart**. This grouping is more useful than memorizing one long alphabetical list.

For search pages, add examples that show the user’s next move. A crossword solver should set length. A writer should compare tone. A word-game player should verify the dictionary. Those reminders make the page safer and more useful because RE words are common enough to tempt overconfidence.

For learners, RE words are also a good way to talk about habits. **Reread** teaches study strategy, **retry** teaches persistence, and **repair** teaches restoration. That makes the page useful beyond games: it can support vocabulary, classroom discussion, and writing prompts about improvement or second attempts.
