articles.longform_markdown:
## Why PRE words are useful

Words starting with **pre** are useful because they often point to something that happens before, earlier, or in advance. **Preheat** means heat before cooking. **Prepay** means pay before a due time. **Preview** means view before the full version. That “before” idea appears in school, planning, publishing, cooking, technology, and everyday instructions.

A user may search PRE words for a prefix lesson, a crossword clue, a word-game candidate, or a writing choice. The list gives a focused family, but the best next step depends on the job. Use [Words Starting With](/word-tools/words-starting-with/) for live length filters and [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/) when rack letters or fixed positions matter.

PRE is less general than RE, but it is still productive. It often makes a word feel organized, planned, or formal, which is useful in writing but can sound stiff if overused.

## What PRE usually means

The main meaning of **pre-** is **before**. That can mean before in time, as in **preheat** or **prepay**. It can mean before in order, as in **prefix**, where something is fixed before another part. It can mean before full release or full experience, as in **preview**.

Some PRE words are clear even to beginners because the base word is obvious: **pretest**, **precut**, **prewash**, **preplan**. Others are less transparent because the word has a longer history or specialized use. A starts-with list will include both kinds when they match the letters.

The base-word test helps. If removing PRE leaves a recognizable word and the meaning is “before that action,” the prefix is probably doing visible work. If not, treat the word as a spelling match first and a prefix example only after checking context.

## PRE words in school, work, and writing

PRE words are common in instructions and planning language. Teachers use **pretest**, **preview**, and **prewrite**. Workplaces use **preplan**, **preapprove**, and **preselect**. Recipes use **preheat**. Publishing and media use **preview** and **preorder**. These words save space because the before-meaning is built in.

For writing, PRE words can sound precise. “Prepay the fee” is shorter than “pay the fee before the deadline.” “Preview the chapter” is clearer than “look at the chapter before reading it fully.” But too many PRE words can sound bureaucratic. Choose them when the before-meaning matters.

In a classroom, PRE words are excellent for showing how prefixes connect to real actions. Students can act out **preheat**, **precut**, and **preview** because the before sequence is visible.

## PRE words in crosswords and word games

Crossword clues may signal PRE with words such as “before,” “advance,” “early,” “prior,” or “prepare.” If the answer starts with PRE, set the exact length before scanning. A five-letter PRE word has very different possibilities from a nine-letter PRE word.

For word games, PRE can be a useful start, but game legality and board fit must be checked. A word may appear in a broad dictionary and still be irrelevant to the current game. A high-scoring candidate may fail because there is no place to put it. Use the PRE list as study material, then use the appropriate game helper for actual play.

If the clue gives fixed letters after PRE, use pattern mode. A pattern such as **pre??t** asks a more exact question than simply browsing all PRE words.

## PRE vs RE and ANTE

PRE and RE can look similar but point to different time directions. **Prepay** means pay before. **Repay** means pay back. **Preview** means view before the full thing. **Review** can mean view again or examine. A single extra letter changes the time relationship.

PRE and ANTE both relate to before, but PRE is more productive in everyday English. ANTE appears in words such as **antecedent** and **antedate**, and in game language as a stake placed before play. For most basic vocabulary and puzzle work, PRE is the more useful prefix family to learn first.

To compare prefix families, read [What Prefixes Do in English](/guides/what-prefixes-do-in-english/) and [Common English Word Beginnings](/guides/common-english-word-beginnings/).

## Common mistakes with PRE words

Do not assume every word beginning **pre** is a beginner-friendly prefix example. Some are borrowed or specialized words where the modern base is not obvious. Use transparent examples first when teaching.

Do not confuse ordinary writing with game-list acceptance. A form may be understandable in a sentence but not accepted by a specific game dictionary. Hyphenation can also differ between prose and game lists, which usually exclude hyphenated compounds.

Do not use PRE just because it sounds formal. **Plan early** may be clearer than **preplan** in some sentences. The prefix should serve meaning, not decorate the word.

## A practical PRE workflow

For vocabulary, start with concrete actions: **preheat**, **prepay**, **preview**, **precut**, **pretest**. Ask what happens first and what happens later. Then move to abstract words such as **predict** or **prepare** only after the basic before idea is clear.

For crosswords, search PRE with exact length and read the clue carefully. If the clue says “beforehand,” PRE may be likely. If the clue gives internal letters, use [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/) pattern mode.

For writing, choose PRE words when the before-meaning is central. If the prefix does not add clarity, a simpler phrase may read better. For additional prefix families, compare the UN and RE word pages: [Words Starting With UN](/word-tools/words-starting-with-un/) and [Words Starting With RE](/word-tools/words-starting-with-re/).

PRE words are especially useful in process writing because they describe preparation. A student can **preview** a chapter, **prewrite** ideas, take a **pretest**, and then revise after feedback. A recipe can tell a cook to **preheat** the oven. A business process can ask for **preapproval** before action.

That real-world layer matters for authority. The page should not only list PRE words; it should show why someone needs them. The user may be preparing a lesson, solving a clue, naming a step in a workflow, or checking a word-game candidate. Each scenario uses the same starting letters but applies different standards.

For learners, PRE words also reinforce sequencing. Ask what happens first and what happens after. In **preheat**, heating comes before cooking. In **preorder**, ordering comes before release or delivery. This before-and-after frame makes the prefix memorable and helps users decide whether PRE is really doing meaning work in a given word.

articles.longform_markdown:
## What counts as a double-letter word

A double-letter word contains the same letter twice in a row: **ee**, **oo**, **ll**, **ss**, **tt**, **ff**, and so on. The repeated letters must be consecutive. **Letter** has **tt**. **Book** has **oo**. **Banana** repeats **a**, but it is not a double-letter example because the repeated letters are not adjacent.

Users search double-letter words for several practical reasons. A word-game player may be stuck with two of the same tile. A crossword solver may know that two middle boxes contain the same letter. A student may be studying spelling rules. A puzzle writer may need examples for a quiz or classroom activity.

The list is useful because double letters are visible. They create patterns you can spot quickly, and patterns make word search easier.

## Vowel doubles and consonant doubles

Vowel doubles include words such as **bee**, **food**, **good**, **moon**, **room**, **soon**, **tool**, and **wood**. These can be helpful when a rack has too many vowels or when a crossword pattern shows repeated vowel positions. Not every long vowel sound is spelled with a doubled vowel, so spelling still needs checking.

Consonant doubles include words such as **add**, **egg**, **ill**, **off**, **bell**, **hill**, **fall**, **fossil**, and **letter**. Some doubled consonants appear inside base words, while others appear when a suffix is added. For example, **run** becomes **running** and **stop** becomes **stopped** in standard spelling.

For teaching, separate these groups. Vowel doubles often raise pronunciation questions. Consonant doubles often raise spelling-rule questions. Sorting examples makes the pattern easier to remember than one mixed list.

## Double letters in crosswords and pattern puzzles

Repeated letters are strong crossword clues because they restrict the pattern. If a five-letter answer looks like **_OO_ _**, the possibilities are much narrower than an unrestricted five-letter word. If a clue has **_LL_**, the double consonant can confirm or reject candidates quickly.

Use [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/) pattern mode when the repeated positions are fixed. Enter known letters and use a single-character wildcard for each unknown slot. If you only know that a word contains **oo** or **ll** somewhere, use [Words Containing](/word-tools/words-containing/) with that exact pair.

For starts and endings, use positional tools. A word beginning with **oo** is a different search from a word containing **oo** anywhere. A word ending in **ss** can be found with [Words Ending With](/word-tools/words-ending-with/). Choose the tool that matches the information you actually have.

## Double letters in word games

A rack with repeated letters can feel limiting, but short double-letter words often help. Words such as **add**, **egg**, **ill**, **off**, **all**, **bee**, and **too** can turn awkward duplicates into playable candidates, depending on the game and board.

Do not judge a double-letter play by word length alone. A short word can be valuable if it creates parallel plays or lands a high-value tile well. A longer word can be weak if it opens the board or misses premium squares. The list provides candidates, not move rankings.

Game validation matters. Some double-letter words are ordinary English, some are rare, and some may not be accepted in a specific game dictionary. Use a verified game helper or game dictionary for final play decisions.

## Spelling lessons from double letters

Double letters often appear because English spelling preserves patterns from word history, pronunciation, or suffix rules. A short stressed word may double the final consonant before a vowel-starting suffix: **run** to **running**, **stop** to **stopped**, **sit** to **sitting**. Longer or differently stressed words may not double in the same way.

Some double letters are simply part of the word: **book**, **food**, **letter**, **fossil**, **committee**. Learners usually memorize these through reading and repeated exposure rather than a single rule. That is why double-letter lists are helpful for practice.

When teaching, ask students to mark the double letters, say the word, and sort by pair. **ee**, **oo**, **ll**, and **ss** groups are easier to remember than a random list.

## Common double-letter mistakes

Do not assume pronunciation tells you spelling. A word may have a long vowel sound without a double vowel, or a doubled consonant without an obvious sound difference. English spelling is visual as well as phonetic.

Do not assume any repeated letter counts. The repeated letters must be adjacent for a double-letter list. **Alabama** repeats **a**, but not as a double. **Balloon** has **ll** and **oo**, so it is a stronger double-letter example.

Do not use a double-letter list as the whole solution for a word game. Board position, tile values, and dictionary rules still matter. A list is a study aid and candidate source.

## A practical double-letter workflow

For crosswords, identify whether the doubled pair is fixed. If yes, use Word Finder pattern mode. If no, search the pair with [Words Containing](/word-tools/words-containing/). Then apply clue meaning and length.

For word games, start with short double-letter words because they are easier to place. Then explore longer candidates when the board allows. If your rack contains duplicate vowels, scan vowel-double examples. If it contains duplicate consonants, scan consonant-double examples.

For spelling, build pair groups: **ee**, **oo**, **ll**, **ss**, **tt**, **ff**. Add examples over time. The goal is not to memorize every word at once, but to recognize repeated-letter patterns quickly when reading, writing, or solving.

For spelling practice, double-letter words work best when learners physically mark the pair. Circle **oo** in **book**, underline **ll** in **bell**, and compare **hoping** with **hopping**. The contrast shows that one letter can change both spelling and meaning. It also prevents learners from treating doubles as random decoration.

For puzzle practice, build patterns from the pair. Search **oo**, then try fixed patterns such as **_oo_** or **b__k** in the appropriate tool. This teaches users why a simple list is only the first step. The real skill is turning a visible double into a search constraint.

For advanced word study, compare single-letter and double-letter pairs: **hoping** versus **hopping**, **later** versus **latter**, **canon** versus **cannon**. These pairs show why double letters are not just visual patterns; they can change pronunciation, meaning, and word identity. That makes double-letter lists useful for proofreading as well as puzzles.

faq.q: What is the difference between double letters and repeated letters?
faq.a: Double letters are adjacent repeated letters, while repeated letters can appear anywhere for practical word study and puzzle work. Balloon has double letters because ll and oo are side by side. Banana repeats a, but it does not contain a double a.

faq.q: Are double-letter words useful in Scrabble or word games?
faq.a: Double-letter words can be useful when your rack contains duplicate tiles in practical word-search and vocabulary work. Short examples may help clear awkward letters, but game legality and board position still decide the actual play. Verify candidates in the correct game list.

faq.q: Why do some words double a consonant before a suffix?
faq.a: Some short stressed words double the final consonant before vowel-starting suffixes in practical word-search and vocabulary work. Run becomes running and stop becomes stopped. The pattern has exceptions, so examples and spelling practice are safer than relying on one rule.

faq.q: Can a word have more than one double-letter pair?
faq.a: Yes, some words contain more than one consecutive repeated pair when the rule and dictionary match. Balloon has ll and oo, and committee has several repeated-letter patterns. These words are useful for spelling practice and pattern recognition.

articles.longform_markdown:
## What “no vowels” means here

On this page, “words with no vowels” means words with no **A, E, I, O, or U**. The letter **Y** is allowed. That definition matches the practical question many puzzle and word-game users are asking: “What can I play or solve when I have no traditional vowel letters?”

This definition needs to be stated because Y often acts like a vowel sound. **Myth**, **crypt**, **rhythm**, and **sky** all rely on Y to carry the syllable. If you define vowel by sound, these words are not truly vowel-less. If you define the search by the five traditional vowel letters, they qualify.

A user searching this list is usually in a bind. Their rack is full of consonants, their puzzle answer has no A/E/I/O/U, or they are curious whether English words can exist without the usual vowel letters. The list helps, but the definition matters.

## Why Y matters so much

Most useful no-vowel words contain **Y**. That is not an accident. English words usually need a vowel sound, and Y often supplies it. In **my**, **shy**, **sky**, **ply**, **crypt**, and **rhythm**, the letter Y does work that a traditional vowel might do in another word.

For word-game players, Y can rescue a consonant-heavy rack. It pairs with common consonants and makes short playable-looking candidates easier to find. For crossword solvers, Y can explain how an answer appears to have no vowels while still being pronounceable.

For spelling lessons, no-vowel words are a good way to teach that “vowel letter” and “vowel sound” are different ideas. The letters A, E, I, O, and U are traditional vowel letters. Y is a sometimes-vowel because its role changes by word.

## Short no-vowel words to learn first

Start with short examples because they are easiest to use in puzzles and games: **by**, **my**, **shy**, **sky**, **ply**, **sly**, **wry**, and **why**. These words are familiar and show how Y supports pronunciation.

Then learn a few high-value longer examples: **myth**, **myths**, **crypt**, **glyph**, **nymph**, **nymphs**, **rhythm**, and **hymn**. Some of these are common vocabulary; others are more puzzle-like. The goal is not to memorize every rare word first. It is to build a small toolkit that helps when no traditional vowels are available.

Specialty words such as **cwm** and **crwth** appear in many word-list discussions because they are unusual and useful in games. They may be unfamiliar in ordinary writing, so treat them as puzzle vocabulary rather than everyday prose.

## No-vowel words in word games

A consonant-heavy rack is frustrating because many English words need at least one traditional vowel. No-vowel words give you escape routes. Short words can clear tiles, connect to existing board letters, or buy time without exchanging.

But the word list is only one part of the decision. Board position, tile values, premium squares, and the selected dictionary all matter. A rare word may be accepted in one game list and rejected in another. A short word may score better than a longer one if it creates parallel plays.

Use [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/) when you have an exact rack. Enter your letters and choose the appropriate list mode. Use this page to study the family of no-vowel words so you recognize them faster when they appear.

## No-vowel words in crosswords and word puzzles

Crossword clues sometimes ask directly for words “without vowels,” but more often the pattern reveals it. If crossings create a word with no A/E/I/O/U, look for Y or specialty words. The answer may be common, such as **myth**, or puzzle-heavy, such as **cwm**.

For fixed patterns, use Word Finder pattern mode. If the answer is five letters and the second letter is Y, enter the known positions and wildcards. If you only know a sequence such as **yth**, use [Words Containing](/word-tools/words-containing/) to find words with that exact cluster.

In Wordle-style practice, no-vowel words are useful reminders that A/E/I/O/U are not the only letters that can carry a word. Still, every puzzle has its own answer-list rules, so do not assume every valid dictionary word can be an answer.

## Common mistakes with no-vowel words

The biggest mistake is arguing from the wrong definition. If someone counts Y as a vowel, then many examples on this page are not vowel-less by sound. If the task asks for no A/E/I/O/U, then Y words are exactly the point. State the rule before judging the list.

Another mistake is ignoring familiar short words. Players often jump to exotic examples and forget **by** or **my**. Short words can be more useful than rare long ones because they fit more boards and puzzles.

A third mistake is using rare examples in ordinary writing where they distract the reader. **Crwth** may be a valuable word-game word, but it will not fit most casual sentences. Use no-vowel lists for the task at hand.

## A practical no-vowel workflow

For games, memorize a starter set: **by**, **my**, **shy**, **sky**, **ply**, **wry**, **myth**, **crypt**, **rhythm**. Then use Word Finder for exact racks. Over time, add rarer examples only if they appear in your game’s accepted list.

For crosswords, trust the crossings. If the pattern has no traditional vowels, search with known positions rather than guessing. For spelling lessons, compare vowel letters with vowel sounds and ask what job Y is doing.

For related contrast, visit [Words With All Five Vowels](/word-tools/words-with-all-vowels/). No-vowel words and all-vowel words are opposite extremes, and both teach the same lesson: letter patterns matter only when the definition is clear.

For a practical study set, divide no-vowel words into familiar, game-focused, and rare. Familiar words include **by**, **my**, **sky**, **shy**, and **why**. Game-focused examples include words a player might memorize for difficult racks. Rare examples belong in a separate group so they do not distract beginners.

That grouping keeps the page useful for more than trivia. Beginners get a starter list they can actually remember. Experienced players can study harder candidates. Teachers can use the same page to explain why Y behaves differently from the five traditional vowel letters.

faq.q: What are words with no vowels?
faq.a: Words with no vowels usually means words without A, E, I, O, or U in common modern English usage and examples. In this page’s practical puzzle definition, Y is allowed, so words like my, sky, myth, and rhythm qualify.

faq.q: Is Y a vowel in no-vowel words?
faq.a: Y can act like a vowel sound, but it is not one of the five traditional vowel letters. Many no-vowel word lists allow Y because users are looking for words without A, E, I, O, or U.

faq.q: How do I find no-vowel words from my letters?
faq.a: Use Word Finder with your exact rack letters, especially if you have Y when choosing the correct search workflow. A no-vowel list helps you study patterns, but the finder checks what can be made from the actual letters you have.

faq.q: Are no-vowel words useful in Scrabble or word games?
faq.a: No-vowel words are useful when your rack is full of consonants for practical word study and puzzle work. Short examples can clear awkward tiles, but validity depends on the game dictionary and board position. Verify rare words before playing them.

faq.q: What is the difference between no vowels and no vowel sounds?
faq.a: No vowels usually refers to missing vowel letters, while no vowel sounds refers to pronunciation. Myth has no A/E/I/O/U, but Y supplies a vowel sound. The intended definition changes which words count.

faq.q: Why are many no-vowel words rare?
faq.a: English usually needs vowel sounds, so words without traditional vowel letters often rely on Y or come from specialized sources. Many are puzzle-friendly but uncommon in everyday writing. Learn familiar short examples first, then add rare words only when your game or puzzle accepts them.

articles.longform_markdown:
## What “all five vowels” means

On this page, “words with all five vowels” means words that contain at least one **A**, **E**, **I**, **O**, and **U**. The vowels do not have to appear in alphabetical order unless a puzzle specifically says they must. Extra vowels may appear too. The key requirement is that all five traditional vowel letters are present somewhere in the word.

That definition matters because people use “all vowels” in different ways. Some puzzles ask for words containing A, E, I, O, and U in any order. Others ask for the vowels in order. Others ask for each vowel exactly once. Those are different constraints and produce different answer sets.

A user searching this list may have a vowel-heavy word-game rack, a trivia clue, a classroom activity, or a word curiosity question. The list helps identify candidates, but the rule must be clear before deciding whether a word qualifies.

## Why all-vowel words are useful

All-five-vowel words are rare enough to feel special. They are useful in vocabulary puzzles because they satisfy a visible constraint. They also help word-game players think about vowel management. A rack with many vowels can be hard to play, and words that use several different vowels can clear space.

For teachers, all-vowel words show how vowels distribute across longer words. They invite questions about syllables, spelling, and pronunciation. For writers, they can be used in constrained prompts, wordplay, or titles where the visible vowel pattern matters.

These words are also good for teaching precision. “Contains all five vowels” is not the same as “contains only vowels.” Most all-five-vowel words include consonants too. It is also not the same as “has vowels in order.”

## Any order, in order, or exactly once

The most common definition is **any order**: a word qualifies if A, E, I, O, and U all appear somewhere. A word can contain repeated vowels and still qualify. This is the broadest and most practical version for general word lists.

A stricter puzzle may ask for vowels **in order**: A before E before I before O before U. That is a pattern problem, not just a membership problem. The word must contain the vowels in that sequence, though consonants and sometimes extra vowels may appear between them depending on the puzzle’s rule.

An even stricter puzzle may ask for each vowel **exactly once**. That eliminates words with extra A, E, I, O, or U letters. Before using any list, check which version the page or puzzle expects. Many disagreements about all-vowel words come from mixing definitions.

## All-vowel words in word games

A vowel-heavy rack can be just as awkward as a consonant-heavy rack. All-five-vowel words can teach useful patterns, but real play depends on the exact letters and board. A long word that contains every vowel may be impossible from your rack, while a shorter word using three vowels may be the better move.

Use [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/) when you have actual rack letters. It can test what can be made from those letters within your selected length range or pattern. Use this all-vowel page for study, recognition, and puzzle curiosity.

Game validity still matters. Some unusual all-vowel examples may appear in one word list and not another. Board placement, hooks, and premium squares determine the value of a play more than the fact that every vowel appears.

## All-vowel words in crosswords and trivia

Crossword clues about all five vowels are often meta clues. Instead of giving an ordinary definition, the clue may ask for a word that “contains every vowel” or “uses A, E, I, O, and U.” In that case, the letter property is the clue.

Length and crossings are critical. If the answer has seven letters, the list is very different from a twelve-letter answer. If one crossing gives Q or X, that may point toward a smaller set of candidates. When exact positions are known, use Word Finder pattern mode rather than scanning a broad list.

For a known sequence, use [Words Containing](/word-tools/words-containing/). If a puzzle asks for **aei** or **eou** as adjacent letters, that is a substring search. If it only asks for all vowels anywhere, a membership-style list or rack finder is a better fit.

## Common mistakes with all-vowel words

Do not assume the vowels must be in A-E-I-O-U order. That is a special puzzle condition, not the default. The default list usually means all five appear somewhere.

Do not assume the word is made only of vowels. **All five vowels** means each vowel appears, not that consonants are excluded. Words with only vowels are a different and much smaller idea.

Do not ignore repeated vowels. If the rule says “contains all five vowels,” repeated vowels are normally allowed. If the rule says “each vowel exactly once,” repeated vowels are not allowed. The wording controls the answer.

Do not use rare words in writing only because they satisfy the pattern. A word may be interesting but distracting. Choose it when the vowel constraint is part of the task.

## A practical all-vowel workflow

First, define the rule: any order, in order, or exactly once. Second, define the task: puzzle answer, rack play, classroom list, or writing prompt. Third, choose the tool. Use Word Finder for available letters or fixed positions, [Words Containing](/word-tools/words-containing/) for adjacent sequences, and the all-vowel list for broad study.

For a classroom activity, compare this page with [Words With No Vowels](/word-tools/words-with-no-vowels/). The two lists show opposite extremes: one requires every traditional vowel, the other excludes A/E/I/O/U. Together they help students see that letter rules must be stated clearly.

For crosswords and games, use all-vowel words as candidates, not guarantees. The final answer must fit the clue, length, crossings, board, and dictionary.

For practice, ask users to label the rule before giving answers. “All five vowels anywhere” is the easiest version. “All five vowels in order” is stricter. “Each vowel exactly once” is stricter still. A word may be correct under one rule and wrong under another.

This rule-first habit is valuable in crosswords, classrooms, and word games. It prevents debates that come from mismatched definitions. It also helps users choose the right tool: a broad list for curiosity, [Words Containing](/word-tools/words-containing/) for an adjacent sequence, or [Word Finder](/word-tools/word-finder/) for exact letters and positions.

faq.q: What are words with all five vowels?
faq.a: Words with all five vowels contain at least one A, E, I, O, and U in practical word-list searches. The vowels usually can appear in any order unless the puzzle specifically requires a sequence. Extra vowels are normally allowed unless the rule says each vowel appears exactly once.

faq.q: Do all five vowels have to be in order?
faq.a: No, all five vowels do not have to be in order unless the rule says so. Any-order lists only require A, E, I, O, and U to appear somewhere in the word. Order-specific puzzles should say A-E-I-O-U or use similar wording.

faq.q: How do I find words with all vowels from my letters?
faq.a: Use Word Finder when you have an exact rack or set of available letters when choosing the correct search workflow. The all-vowel list teaches patterns, but a finder checks whether your specific letters can actually make a word.

faq.q: What is the difference between all-vowel words and words made only of vowels?
faq.a: All-vowel words contain every traditional vowel, but they can include consonants in practical word-search and vocabulary work. Words made only of vowels use no consonants at all. These are different constraints and should not be mixed in puzzles.

faq.q: Are all-vowel words useful in Scrabble or word games?
faq.a: All-vowel words can help with vowel-heavy racks, but they are not automatically playable for practical word study and puzzle work. Game dictionary, rack letters, board placement, and scoring opportunities decide whether a candidate is useful. Verify specific plays before using them.

faq.q: Why does my all-vowel answer not match a puzzle?
faq.a: The puzzle may require a stricter rule, such as vowels in order or each vowel exactly once. Recheck the clue wording, answer length, and crossings. Different all-vowel definitions produce different valid answers, so the rule matters as much as the word itself.
